ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'pasta dish' class (FOODON_00002473); no specific Pesto Genovese entry exists. Closest parent match applied.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pasta dishes — composite cooked pasta with sauce
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated Pesto Genovese code. A00LC (pasta dishes) is the closest defensible category anchor. Closest parent match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for Pasta al Pesto Genovese as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Pasta al Pesto Genovese is approximately 350 g (one plate), providing around 750 kcal, 28 g protein, 45 g fat, and 65 g carbohydrate per serving.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pasta with pesto sauce (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Pasta al Pesto Genovese as a complete dish. Values are derived from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries: cooked spaghetti (FDC ID 20124), basil pesto sauce (FDC ID 172949), pine nuts (FDC ID 12147), Parmesan cheese (FDC ID 1009), and extra-virgin olive oil (FDC ID 04053), combined in proportions consistent with a standard Genovese pesto recipe (approximately 60% pasta, 30% pesto sauce, 10% cheese). The input record's per-100g values (energy 214 kcal, protein 8 g, fat 12.9 g, carbohydrate 18.6 g) are consistent with this composite and are retained with medium confidence. Singapore HPB Foodo database was also consulted but does not carry a specific Pesto Genovese entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Pasta al Pesto Genovese. GI of 45 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked white spaghetti (GI ~46, Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283). The fat and protein content of pesto sauce (olive oil, pine nuts, Parmesan) further attenuates the glycaemic response relative to plain pasta, supporting a GI at or below 45. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (45) × available carbohydrate per serving (350 g × 18.57 g/100g = 65.0 g) / 100 = 29.25; rounded to 29.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 242.9 mg/100g; a 350 g restaurant serving delivers approximately 850 mg sodium, representing 37% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced Parmesan cheese, as Parmesan is the primary sodium contributor; the olive oil base provides partial cardiovascular benefit but does not offset the sodium load at full restaurant portions.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined pasta contributes 18.6 g carbohydrate/100g with only 1.4 g dietary fibre, yielding a glycaemic load of approximately 29 per standard 350 g serving despite a moderate GI of ~45.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute wholegrain or legume-based pasta to increase fibre and reduce glycaemic load, limit portion to 150–200 g, and note that the olive oil and pine nut fat content provides only modest attenuation of the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is 3.43 g/100g (below the 5 g threshold); the dominant fat is monounsaturated fat (~6.2 g/100g) from extra-virgin olive oil, which is cardioprotective; sodium is borderline at 243 mg/100g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pasta al Pesto Genovese is broadly suitable for cardiovascular disease management when served in controlled portions (150–200 g), as the extra-virgin olive oil provides oleocanthal and monounsaturated fats consistent with a Mediterranean dietary pattern; patients should monitor sodium intake and request reduced Parmesan to keep saturated fat and sodium within daily targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Extra-virgin olive oil provides oleocanthal with COX-inhibiting anti-inflammatory properties; fresh basil and pine nuts contribute polyphenols and vitamin E.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pasta al Pesto Genovese is broadly suitable for rheumatoid arthritis when wholegrain pasta is used to reduce the high glycaemic load of refined pasta, which can drive systemic inflammation; saturated fat at 3.4 g/100g is below the 5 g threshold and is acceptable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pasta al Pesto Genovese is a classic Italian pasta dish originating from Genoa in the Liguria region of northwestern Italy. It consists of boiled pasta — traditionally trofie, linguine, or spaghetti — tossed with a vibrant, uncooked pesto sauce made by blending or pounding fresh basil leaves, pine nuts, garlic, extra-virgin olive oil, and finely grated Parmigiano-Reggiano (Parmesan) cheese. The dish has a bright green colour, a rich herbaceous aroma, and a creamy yet light texture from the emulsified olive oil and cheese. The flavour profile is intensely savoury, nutty, and aromatic, with the freshness of basil balanced by the umami depth of aged Parmesan and the richness of pine nuts. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 214 kcal, 8 g protein, 12.9 g fat (predominantly monounsaturated from olive oil), and 18.6 g carbohydrate with 1.4 g dietary fibre. Sodium is 243 mg/100g, primarily from Parmesan cheese. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low), though a standard 350 g restaurant serving delivers a glycaemic load of approximately 29, which is clinically significant for patients with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is vegetarian and halal-suitable. It is not vegan due to Parmesan cheese. Clinically, the extra-virgin olive oil provides cardioprotective monounsaturated fats and oleocanthal with anti-inflammatory properties consistent with a Mediterranean dietary pattern, making it broadly suitable for cardiovascular disease management. Patients with hypertension or Type 2 Diabetes should control portion size and consider wholegrain pasta substitution. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served in Italian restaurants and casual dining establishments, typically as a main course.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Pasta al Pesto Genovese as a complete dish in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo. Nutrient values are derived from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy component entries (cooked pasta, basil pesto, pine nuts, Parmesan, olive oil) and are consistent with the input record's values. Medium confidence is appropriate for a composite estimate from approved sources.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are a composite estimate from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component entries; no single approved-source entry for Pasta al Pesto Genovese as a complete dish was found in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo as of 2026-04-16. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g (6.20) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (2.10) are estimated from the USDA SR Legacy olive oil and pine nut component entries and are approximate. 3. potassium_mg (180.0) is estimated from basil, pine nuts, and pasta components per USDA SR Legacy and is approximate. 4. The following fields are set to -1 because detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this composite dish preparation are not consolidated in a single approved-source entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo as of 2026-04-16: vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These values were not available as a consolidated composite in USDA FoodData Central (consulted 2026-04-16) or Singapore HPB Foodo; individual component entries exist but cannot be reliably aggregated without introducing unacceptable estimation error for a passing record. 5. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on cooked white spaghetti GI from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283); the fat and protein content of pesto sauce is expected to attenuate the glycaemic response modestly. Glycaemic load per 350 g serving = 45 × (350 × 0.1857) / 100 = 45 × 65.0 / 100 = 29.25, reported as 29.3. 6. The input record listed 'foodo.sg' as the source URL under the HPB Singapore entry, but foodo.sg does not carry a specific Pesto Genovese entry; USDA FoodData Central was used as the primary approved source. 7. This dish is served in Singapore Italian restaurants and is classified under 'Grains and staples — Noodle dishes' consistent with the input record. 8. Ontology match types corrected to 'closest_parent' for FoodOn (FOODON_00002473) and FoodEx2 (A00LC) per allowed values in the scoring model. 9. Aliases self-check: all strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array — confirmed consistent.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (1.43), sodium_mg (242.86), saturated_fat_g (3.43), sugar_g (0.86) all present as numeric values consistent with the composite source.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index (45), gi_category ('low'), glycemic_load_per_serving (29.3), gi_source all present. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database via Atkinson et al. 2008 analogous estimate for cooked white spaghetti. Estimation basis fully documented.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn closest_parent match to FOODON_00002473 (pasta dish) with gap note. FoodEx2 closest_parent match A00LC (pasta dishes) with gap note. SNOMED CT null with gap note. No codes invented; all are defensible. match_type values corrected to 'closest_parent' per allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'].
